message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Background</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Exponential-like infection growths leading to peaks (which could be the inflection points or turning points) are usually the hallmarks of infectious disease outbreaks including coronaviruses. To predict the inflection points,
                    <jats:italic>i.e</jats:italic>
                    ., inflection time (
                    <jats:italic>T</jats:italic>
                    <jats:sub>max</jats:sub>
                    ) & maximal infection number (
                    <jats:italic>I</jats:italic>
                    <jats:sub>max</jats:sub>
                    ) of the novel coronavirus (COVID-19), we adopted a trial and error strategy and explored a series of approaches from simple
                    <jats:italic>logistic modeling</jats:italic>
                    (that has an asymptomatic line) to sophisticated
                    <jats:italic>tipping point</jats:italic>
                    detection techniques for detecting phase transitions but failed to obtain satisfactory results.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Method</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Inspired by its success in diversity-time relationship (DTR), we apply the PLEC (power law with exponential cutoff) model for detecting the inflection points of COVID-19 outbreaks. The model was previously used to extend the classic species-time relationship (STR) for general DTR (Ma 2018), and it has two “secondary” parameters (computed from its 3 parameters including power law scaling parameter
                    <jats:italic>w</jats:italic>
                    , taper-off parameter
                    <jats:italic>d</jats:italic>
                    to overwhelm virtually exponential growth ultimately, and a parameter
                    <jats:italic>c</jats:italic>
                    related to initial infections): one that was originally used for estimating the potential or ‘dark’ biodiversity is proposed to estimate the
                    <jats:italic>maximal infection number</jats:italic>
                    (
                    <jats:italic>I</jats:italic>
                    <jats:sub>
                      <jats:italic>max</jats:italic>
                    </jats:sub>
                    ) and another is proposed to determine the corresponding
                    <jats:italic>inflection time point</jats:italic>
                    (
                    <jats:italic>T</jats:italic>
                    <jats:sub>
                      <jats:italic>max</jats:italic>
                    </jats:sub>
                    ).
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    We successfully estimated the inflection points [
                    <jats:italic>I</jats:italic>
                    <jats:sub>max</jats:sub>
                    ,
                    <jats:italic>T</jats:italic>
                    <jats:sub>max</jats:sub>
                    ] for most provinces (≈85%) in China with error rates <5% in both
                    <jats:italic>I</jats:italic>
                    <jats:sub>
                      <jats:italic>max</jats:italic>
                    </jats:sub>
                    and
                    <jats:italic>T</jats:italic>
                    <jats:sub>
                      <jats:italic>max</jats:italic>
                    </jats:sub>
                    . We also discussed the constraints and limitations of the proposed approach, including (
                    <jats:italic>i</jats:italic>
                    ) sensitive to disruptive jumps, (
                    <jats:italic>ii</jats:italic>
                    ) requiring sufficiently long datasets, and (
                    <jats:italic>iii</jats:italic>
                    ) limited to unimodal outbreaks.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: A Simple Mathematical Model for Estimating the Inflection Points of COVID-19 Outbreaks

reference.unstructured: Bates DM , Watts DG (1988) Nonlinear Regression Analysis and Its Applications, Wiley

reference.article-title: DAR (diversity–area relationship): extending classic SAR (species–area relationship) for biodiversity and biogeography analyses
Diversity time-period and diversity-time-area relationships exemplified by the human microbiome
A new DTAR (diversity–time–area relationship) model demonstrated with the indoor microbiome

reference.unstructured: Ma ZS (2020) Spatiotemporal fluctuation scaling law and metapopulation modeling of the novel coronavirus (COVID-19) and SARS outbreaks. Preprint available at: https://arxiv.org/abs/2003.03714
Ma ZE , YC Zhou , JH Wu (2009) Modeling and Dynamics of Infectious Diseases. World Scientific.

reference.article-title: Using “outbreak science” to strengthen the use of models during epidemics

reference.journal-title: Annual Review of Ecology, Evolution, and Systematics

reference.unstructured: Thompson R. (2020) Pandemic potential of 2019-nCoV. Lancet, DOI: https://doi.org/10.1016/S1473-3099(20)30068-2

reference.article-title: Self-similarity and the species–area relation of Polish butterflies

reference.unstructured: Karunanithi AT , Cabezas H , Frieden BR , et al (2008) Detection and Assessment of Ecosystem Regime Shifts from Fisher Information. Ecology and Society, vol. 13(1)
